finding: Items forgotten via inhibition during retrieval practice benefit disproportionately from subsequent relearning, recovering to higher final accessibility than non-inhibited items. Forgetting paradoxically enhances learning effectiveness by reducing initial accessibility, enabling greater retrieval-strength gains during retraining.

Intermixed presentation of similar stimuli produces superior discrimination compared to blocked presentation independent of trial spacing, implicating attention-based feature differentiation rather than consolidation. Similarity of intermixed items is critical; alternation with dissimilar items produces no advantage.

Phonologically related irrelevant sounds disrupt free recall but not serial recall of phonologically similar to-be-remembered items, with increased intrusion of irrelevant items. Effect emerges when category-cueing processes drive retrieval, not when temporal-order processes dominate.

Semantic interference persists in delayed naming despite elimination of frequency effects, indicating postlexical origin independent of lexical selection bottleneck. Response exclusion from single-channel output buffer, not lexical competition, produces semantic interference.

Reading pathway emphasis changes on trial-by-trial basis when exception words and nonwords alternate unpredictably, with larger switch costs attributable to stimulus-driven exogenous control. Regular words accommodate either pathway when context permits, eliminating switch costs.

Semantic neighborhood effects reverse by neighbor distance: near neighbors slow word recognition via competitive settling interference, distant neighbors speed recognition via gradient facilitation. Attractor dynamics model explains simultaneous inhibitory and facilitative effects within single processing framework.
